chapters.title: A hidden guide inside us points to our favorite brands
chapters.text: Most ads fade fast, yet a few stay with people for years. The Cadbury chocolate campaign earned many creative awards. In the spot, a gorilla listens to music, then drums the beat. The candy bar appears only at the end. By spotlighting one brand, Cadbury turned into a marketing success. The reason some campaigns win is not easy to explain. Neuroeconomist Peter Kenning ran a study showing that people often buy on gut feel. They will reach for a familiar option, even when many choices are on the shelf. The brain leans on what feels known. Psychologist Daniel Kahneman, a founder of behavioral economics and a Nobel winner, says two systems drive our choices. One system handles perception and gut reactions, sorting input by feel. The other handles careful thought. Strong brands are read by the first system. Smart marketers learn to switch on that system for buyers. The second system burns energy, so people default to the first. Think of the gut system as the autopilot and the thinking system as the pilot. Marketer Phil Barden spent 25 years studying how shoppers pick one brand over another. Knowing the buyer and shaping a product to fit their needs sits at the heart of good marketing. Barden went on to build fresh methods for brand positioning and ad design. Brand mentions and ad views climbed. His growing interest in buyer psychology led him to join Decode, a research firm founded by a scientist of decision-making. Barden chose to share what he learned about the link between choice and marketing, and to map the real forces behind a purchase. Every product carries a soft value, what experts call brand equity. People pay a lot for coffee at Starbucks, then return again and again. Coffee is part of it, but something else is too. Once we look at shopping through the lens of neuroscience and cognitive psychology, the hidden reasons for buying start to show. Decoded lays out the science of why we buy. You will see why love of a brand can beat product quality, which visual cues grab the eye, and how framing shifts what we notice.

chapters.title: The mood a brand builds can matter more than the offer
chapters.text: The autopilot system takes in the world through the senses. A new skill needs focus at first, then becomes automatic over time. The autopilot handles a heavy load of input. When you land on a website, your brain scans color, layout, and feel in a flash. The marketing message has to land fast. It must stick, draw you in, and stay short. Your first impression of a brand forms in about one second. The autopilot learns from every signal that comes in. A child hears the word no in a sharp tone and files that sound away. When a pattern repeats, the brain marks it as a likely signal of what comes next. Specific images, words, or sounds in ads build links in the mind. Shoppers spend long stretches in stores and pick what feels right to them. The setting around a choice also shapes it. In one test, people helped each other more often in stores that smelled of fresh bread or coffee. The framing effect is real. Back to Starbucks, why do people pay more for the same drink. The mood of warmth, the music, the room itself, all of it adds up. The framing a brand lays down can pull people back again and again. Marketing and sales teams argue about whether to chase ad quality or brand image. The truth is these need to work together. The brand quietly sets a stage that shapes how the buyer sees value. Most buyers never spot the framing effect at work. The autopilot runs across many brain regions, hidden from thought. The autopilot takes in the world through the five senses, so marketing tactics aim at those senses.

chapters.title: We pay up with a grin when a purchase feels worth it
chapters.text: The framing of a brand sets an expectation in the mind of the buyer. If the buyer views the brand in a warm light, the product will carry that glow. Our view, and so the experience of the product, comes mostly from hidden autopilot work, as Phil Barden has written. When we see a product we want, parts of the brain tied to wanting light up. Add a price, and the lobe tied to pain also switches on. The brain pairs the cost with a small sting. If the pleasure outweighs the pain, we tend to buy. The gap is called net value. Smart brands work to lift the pleasure side so the price feels fair to the buyer. The pleasure from buying should top the sting of spending. The next move is to load the product with extra value. Take the Norwegian water brand Voss, which gets part of its pull from bottle design. People see the water as special because it shows up in high end places. Price shapes the view of value too. Buyers feel the water must be great, because it costs a lot. In one test, people tasted two wines and rated the costlier one higher, but the bottles were the same. Words can lift a product too. The headline, the ad copy, and the label all push us toward a buy. For example, a restaurant dish with a vivid name can make you crave it more. If you want an ad to focus on the gain, try words like discover, get, more, or help. If you want to stop a buyer from losing out, try words like no risk, avoid, without, or free. You can also reshape the price itself by making it feel like a win. Language that points to value can change the real feel. For example, instead of 25 percent fat, say 75 percent meat without fat. Promotional cues, like red numbers or a price tag with a sparkle, push the buyer to think discount. Comparing a product to others that cost more, or putting up sale signs, are two more ways to draw buyers in.

chapters.title: The buyer senses more than meets the eye
chapters.text: Time is another resource buyers care about. Phil Barden shares ways to make waits feel shorter. First, walk the customer to the place where the problem gets explained, and they will sit tight for the fix. Second, stock the waiting area with a TV or books so the time passes. Try to remove the feeling of doubt, and the wait should always pay off. Value also turns on the effort it takes to buy. Smooth paths help a lot. For example, instead of forcing people to sign up on a site before they can buy, let them create an account after, and frame it as a way to speed up future buys. Choice drives demand. You can buy warm water on a hot day, but a cold drink wins every time. We are ready to pay more when we lack a direct sample to compare against. Our sense of value rests on comparing one option to another. Context shapes the value of a product in a strong way. A situation can trigger a memory and pull us toward a specific item. For example, the phrase ice cream brand as a dessert for Christmas narrows the field more than just ice cream brand does. Aim to be number one for a specific situation. Hunt for spots other brands have not claimed. Picture the situations you want tied to your brand, and the sales may grow. Sight plays a big role in choice. We need to see what we plan to buy. Visual focus lands on a small area while the rest fades. Marketers tap into side vision to boost the effect. Walking through a store, you spot the shape and color of a familiar washing powder box from the corner of your eye. You do not need to stop and stare to find it. The product should still look good when blurred. Build a brand that the eye can spot fast.

chapters.title: Your brand does not live outside its setting
chapters.text: The brain does not take in the whole image, but it can spot many objects and pull them into a clear scene. We can pick out the make of a car even when we have never seen that model before. The same skill applies to brands and products. Whatever words you write in the style of the Coca-Cola logo, your mind still hears a Coke drink. When you launch a new product or change a brand look, think about what holds the recognition together. Visual cues like shape, color, and font build the picture. Round logos, for example, often feel more friendly. The brain reads the object and what it means in a flash. Brand cues should match the hopes and goals of the buyer. Brand cues should send clear and warm messages to customers. Barden points to a juice package that had a picture of an orange. After a refresh, the pack showed a glass of juice, meant to signal high status. Sales fell, because that kind of glass shows up in hotels, not in daily home life. The setting of the ad matters too. Take the diamond image, for example. In ads for building materials, a diamond stands for hardness. In ads for hair dye, it stands for shine. The autopilot likes simple input that takes little work to process. Spelling a name in capital letters slows the reader down. The wider the gap between the real price and the marked down price on a tag, the bigger the deal seems. A face is a key tool in marketing too. The pleasure center of the brain lights up at the sight of an attractive face. It does not have to be a person. Animal faces and emojis can pull the same kind of look. We look at faces without thinking. We also tend to look where the person in the ad is looking. When the model looks at the product instead of at us, we tend to grow curious about the product, even if we do not notice it. An umbrella brand is a way to sell several product lines under one name. Big umbrella brands include Apple, Nestle, and P and G.

chapters.title: Change the plan, not the customer
chapters.text: How a product is shown shapes how it is seen. Tweaks in the shopping flow can change choices without changing beliefs. For example, Google revamped its office canteens to nudge staff toward healthy food. The line now starts with salads, and rich desserts are placed in dull containers instead of clear ones. The choices shifted, but the beliefs did not. A behavior shift can spark a self image loop, where past actions shape what we do next. Your view of a healthy salad can change after you pick it for a few days in a row. Here is another example, a man bought flowers for his mother in law after his team won, simply because he was in a good mood. Next time he will buy a bouquet again, since showing up empty handed would feel rude. The buy becomes a habit, mood aside. Most buys happen without a plan. Even when a shopper knows what they want, the layout of the store can change their mind. Three rules guide most shopping trips. First, tangibility. The cues we get should be clear. Paying a monthly gym fee, for example, can push a person to show up more often. Second, speed. The autopilot wants needs met fast. We settle for a small reward today over a bigger one a year from now. Make sure your buyers can taste the result as soon as possible. Third, confidence. We lean toward safe and steady options, and we tend to pick what others seem to pick. The brain runs wanting and liking on different circuits. You can like a cozy bookshop and still order a book from a website. Buyers act on wanting and the hope of a reward, not just on a warm feeling. The more a service matters, the more we will pay. Name the problems your product solves and show clear answers. The value of a product is not how pretty it looks, but how well it meets the buyer goal.

chapters.title: The buyer motive should always point to the goal
chapters.text: Whenever someone mentions a product or service, your brand should be the first that comes to mind. That reach is a key sign of brand health. Goals steer positioning and strategy, so you need to pin down what your product should fulfill. Goals shape what we pay attention to. Goals form and run below the level of thought. We make hundreds of small choices a day. The stronger the link between a brand and a goal, the more likely we are to think of that brand in a given moment. The hope of a reward makes a buy feel rich. In the Bounty ad, a girl tastes a chocolate bar and flowers bloom around her. Buyers know this is a fantasy. Still, they get a real sense of the joy the product can bring. The expectation effect is a strong force. The two main drivers are promotion and prevention. A drill, for example, serves the clear goal of making holes. It also hides a deeper goal, saving effort and lifting output. That second layer hits the need for promotion. Brain science has mapped other universal drives in people, including safety, pleasure, adventure, autonomy, and excitement. Brand promotion should start with clear goals. For example, the top deodorant is the one that best blocks sweat and smell. After that base, brands can reach for hidden goals. In the juice rebranding case, the firm lost a lot of money with the wrong move. The old ad hinted at taking care of the family with fresh juice. The new look missed that promise, and a fancy glass did not carry the warm meaning. Feelings can get in the way of good marketing. We feel strong emotions when we reach a goal. The goals are sharp, but the feelings are wide. Barden suggests positioning brands by goals, not by feelings. We can be creative in how we express the brand, as long as we keep the link between cues and goals strong, as Phil Barden has put it.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text: Reaching the hidden layer of decision-making is a way to give your brand fresh meaning. The skill to read the real goals of buyers and send the right cues helps you understand shoppers. Studying the link between clear and hidden needs sharpens your strategy. When you buy washing powder, you look for clean clothes, but also for a way to care for yourself and your family. Ads should send a cue of protection. Goals give you a sharper way to plan. The skill to read images and patterns in the world on a gut level is the root of human awareness. People from the same culture tend to read cues, ideas, and goals in similar ways. Those links sit below thought. So companies that sell across borders should mind the differences in mindset. When a brand links to many goals, the value of each one fades. Aim at one goal and show that your brand leads the field. Do not lean on emotion alone. A pitch that is only facts or only feelings will miss how the brand can meet a buyer goal. Send clear cues that show the product meets the buyer need. Think like a customer. What do they want when they reach for the kind of product you sell. Hunt for clear and deep motives, and you can build a pitch that truly lands. Try this. Think of the main goal tied to your brand. What hidden need of the buyer should it serve. The choice could be safety, which means caring for the family, or pleasure, which means rest, or excitement, which means fun and curiosity, or adventure, which means freedom and courage, or autonomy, which means success and power, or discipline, which means order and precision. Once you set the goal, strategy and ad cues get easier to pick. If you have read to the end, well done.
